mainEntity.name: What lens is best for real estate photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: For most UK interiors a 24 mm prime on a crop‑sensor body gives a wide view without extreme distortion. In larger rooms a 16‑35 mm zoom works well, while a 35 mm lens is ideal for tighter spaces like bathrooms. Pair the lens with a tripod and keep the aperture around f/8‑f/11 for depth of field.

mainEntity.name: What camera height should I use for property photos?

acceptedAnswer.text: A good rule is to set the camera at about 1.2 metres , roughly eye level for a standing adult. This height mimics how a buyer would view the room and keeps perspective natural. For low‑level features like a coffee table, crouch slightly and keep the horizon low.

mainEntity.name: How do I make small rooms look good without making them look fake?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use a modest wide‑angle lens (around 24 mm) and keep the camera level. Stage the room to show depth , a rug or a line of chairs leads the eye. Avoid excessive distortion by not getting too close to the walls, and correct any keystone in post‑processing.

mainEntity.name: Should I use flash for property photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Flash can help fill shadows, but it often creates harsh spots. If you use flash, bounce it off a ceiling or a reflector to soften the light. In most cases natural light plus a continuous LED panel gives a more realistic result.

mainEntity.name: How much editing should real estate photos have?

acceptedAnswer.text: Editing should enhance clarity, correct colour and straighten verticals. Keep changes subtle , aim for a natural look that matches what a buyer would see in person. Over‑saturation or heavy HDR can make a listing look deceptive.

mainEntity.name: When should I hire a professional property photographer?

acceptedAnswer.text:
Consider hiring a pro if you have high‑value properties, limited time, or want a consistently polished look across many listings. Professional photographers bring specialised lenses, lighting rigs and post‑processing expertise that can boost the perceived value of a property.
